book_claims.claim: Kale should only be consumed if it was part of your ancestral diet; otherwise best avoided or used only occasionally
book_claims.mechanism: Risk of intolerances or autoimmune reactions from non-ancestral foods

book_claims.claim: Significant calcium source: 100 mg per cup raw (10% DV) and 94 mg per cup cooked (9% DV)
book_claims.mechanism: Rich in calcium and fat-soluble vitamins; calcium bioavailability moderate due to oxalate content

supplementary_research.finding: Kale provides sulforaphane precursors and glucosinolates associated with NRF2 pathway activation and cancer-preventive effects; ancestral consumption context is relevant for tolerance
Calcium from kale has ~40% bioavailability compared to ~32% from milk; oxalate is relatively low in kale versus spinach, making it a reasonable non-dairy calcium source

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: ~40% bioavailability; lower oxalate than spinach makes it a better non-dairy calcium source
Exceptionally high K1; fat-soluble, requires dietary fat for absorption
Degrades with prolonged cooking; best lightly steamed or raw

synergies.description: Fat enhances absorption of fat-soluble vitamins K, A, and E from kale
Vitamin C in lemon enhances non-heme iron absorption from kale; brightens flavor
Calcium from kale complements plant protein from legumes in a complete longevity meal
